summary:
This law creates a national initiative called the National Parkinson’s Project, led by the Secretary of Health and Human Services. Its purpose is to prevent, diagnose, treat, and cure Parkinson’s disease and related neurodegenerative disorders, including multiple system atrophy, corticobasal degeneration, progressive supranuclear palsy, and Parkinson’s-related dementia.

The Project requires the Secretary to develop and periodically update an integrated national plan aimed at preventing and treating Parkinson’s, slowing or stopping its progression, and ameliorating symptoms. The Secretary must conduct an annual assessment of the nation’s progress in addressing the growing burden of Parkinson’s, beginning within 24 months of enactment. This assessment must include priority action recommendations and a description of steps taken or planned to implement them. The Secretary must also provide estimates of current federal investment in Parkinson’s efforts and, where applicable, the investment needed. Additional duties include coordinating research and services across federal agencies, encouraging development of safe and effective treatments, improving early diagnosis and care coordination, reviewing the impact on patients and caregivers, and collaborating internationally while avoiding duplication with existing research on related disorders.

The law establishes an Advisory Council on Parkinson’s Research, Care, and Services, composed of federal experts from specified agencies—such as the National Institutes of Health, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and the Department of Veterans Affairs—and ten non-federal members, including patient advocates, a caregiver, health care providers, researchers, and nonprofit representatives. The Council must meet at least quarterly for its first two years, hold an annual research meeting open to the public, and submit an annual report to the Secretary and Congress starting within 18 months of enactment. The report must list federally funded Parkinson’s efforts, recommend priority actions, and evaluate the national plan’s implementation. Federal agencies must share relevant Parkinson’s information with the Secretary to support this report. Both the Advisory Council and the entire section sunset at the end of calendar year 2035.
